front.title: Digital Citizenship Protocol (DCP) for AI Agents

abstract.t: This document specifies the Digital Citizenship Protocol (DCP), a framework for establishing verifiable digital identity, intent declaration, policy governance, and cryptographic audit trails for AI agents operating on open networks. DCP provides post-quantum security through hybrid composite signatures combining classical (Ed25519) and post-quantum (ML-DSA-65) algorithms, ensuring long-term resilience against quantum computing threats.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The proliferation of AI agents operating autonomously on the internet creates a fundamental trust problem: how can humans and systems verify the identity, intent, and behavior of an AI agent? Existing authentication mechanisms (API keys, OAuth tokens) establish access control but not identity or accountability.
DCP addresses this by requiring every AI agent to carry a Citizenship Bundle -- a portable, verifiable package containing:

ul.li: A Responsible Principal Record (RPR) linking the agent to a responsible human or legal entity
An Agent Passport with the agent's identity and capabilities
A cryptographically-chained Audit Trail of all actions taken

section.t: All artifacts are signed with hybrid composite signatures (Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65) providing security against both classical and quantum adversaries.

ul.li: Privacy preservation through blinded identity modes
Interoperability across programming languages and frameworks

section.name: Scope
section.t: This document covers the core DCP protocol (versions 1.0 and 2.0). It does not specify:

ul.li: Specific policy engines or decision algorithms
Network transport requirements beyond wire format

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: An autonomous software entity operating on behalf of a human or organization.
Responsible Principal Record -- links an agent to a responsible entity.
Identity document for an AI agent.
A declared purpose for an action an agent wishes to perform.
An authorization decision for an Intent.
A cryptographically-chained record of an action.
A complete package of DCP artifacts.
A Citizenship Bundle with cryptographic signatures.
A hybrid signature combining classical and post-quantum components.
An adaptive security level determining cryptographic requirements.

section.name: Lifecycle
section.t: The DCP lifecycle for a single agent action proceeds as follows:

ol.li: A human or legal entity creates an RPR and registers an Agent Passport.
Before performing any action, the agent constructs an Intent declaration.
The intent is submitted to a policy engine, which returns a Policy Decision.
The agent executes the action and records an Audit Entry hash-chained to the previous entry.
All artifacts are assembled into a Citizenship Bundle.
The bundle is signed with a composite signature (Ed25519 + ML-DSA-65).
The Signed Bundle is presented to any verifier, who validates it locally.

section.name: Version Negotiation
section.t: Agents and verifiers negotiate protocol version via the dcp_version field present in every artifact. A v2.0 verifier MUST support v1.0 bundles when configured with allow_v1_bundles: true. A v1.0 verifier MUST reject bundles with dcp_version: "2.0" as unsupported.
Capabilities and version support are published at /.well-known/dcp-capabilities.json.

section.name: Responsible Principal Record
section.t: Every agent MUST be bound to a human or legal entity through a Responsible Principal Record (RPR). The RPR establishes accountability for agent actions.
Fields:

dl.dd: Unique identifier for the bound entity. Format: human:<uuid>.
"natural_person" or "legal_entity".
Name of the bound entity.
ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country code of the legal jurisdiction.
Method of identity verification. Values include "government_id", "corporate_registration", "notarized_declaration".
Array of cryptographic key entries (see ).
SHA-256 hash of a pre-shared revocation secret. Revealing the pre-image revokes all keys bound to this RPR.
Protocol version string ("1.0" or "2.0").
256-bit random nonce binding the RPR to a specific session.

section.t: Example (v2.0):

section.name: Blinded RPR (Privacy Mode)
section.t: When privacy is required, PII fields (entity_name, contact information) are replaced by a commitment hash:
Non-PII fields (keys, jurisdiction, liability tier) are preserved for verification. The full RPR MAY be disclosed to authorized parties on demand for regulatory compliance.

section.name: Agent Passport
section.t: Each agent carries a passport signed by its bound human.
Fields:

dl.dd: Unique agent identifier. Format: agent:<name-or-uuid>.
Human-readable name.
Array of declared capabilities (e.g., "web_search", "data_analysis", "negotiate").
SHA-256 hash of the canonical JSON of the owner's RPR.
Array of cryptographic key entries.
"active", "suspended", or "revoked".
Protocol version string.
Session binding nonce.

section.name: Jurisdiction Attestation
section.t: An optional but RECOMMENDED attestation by a government or accredited authority certifying the identity in the RPR:

section.name: Key Identifier Derivation
section.t: Key identifiers (kid) are derived deterministically:
This produces a 128-bit collision-resistant identifier that is unique, deterministic, and reproducible across implementations.

section.name: Intent Declaration
section.t: Before performing any action, an agent MUST declare its intent. The intent is a structured description of the planned action, its target, and its risk profile.
Fields:

dl.dd: Unique intent identifier. Format: intent:<uuid>.
The declaring agent's identifier.
Type of intended action (e.g., "api_call", "web_scrape", "data_retrieval", "initiate_payment", "negotiate").
Target of the action (URL, API endpoint, agent ID, etc.).
Array of data classifications involved (e.g., "public", "pii", "financial_data", "health_data", "credentials", "children_data").
Integer risk assessment from 0 to 1000. Higher values indicate greater risk.
Computed or explicitly declared security tier ("routine", "standard", "elevated", "maximum").
Protocol version string.
Session binding nonce.

section.name: Policy Decision
section.t: Each intent receives a policy decision from a policy engine. The policy engine is external to DCP; the protocol specifies only the decision format.
Fields:

dl.dd: "approve", "escalate", or "block".
SHA-256 hash of the canonical JSON of the intent.
Array of conditions for approval (e.g., "rate_limit: 100/hour", "require_human_confirmation").
Final security tier after verifier policy application.
Protocol version string.
Session binding nonce.

section.name: Adaptive Security Tiers
section.t: The v2.0 security tier system automatically selects cryptographic protection levels based on intent risk:
Tier selection algorithm:
section.sourcecode:
function computeSecurityTier(intent): score = intent.risk_score ?? 0 has_high_value = intent.data_classes INTERSECT {credentials, children_data} != EMPTY has_sensitive = intent.data_classes INTERSECT {pii, financial_data, health_data, credentials, children_data} != EMPTY is_payment = intent.action_type == "initiate_payment"
if score >= 800 OR has_high_value: return "maximum" if score >= 500 OR has_sensitive OR is_payment: return "elevated" if score >= 200: return "standard" return "routine"
section.t: The verifier MAY upgrade the computed tier but MUST NOT downgrade it.

section.name: Hash-Chained Audit Entries
section.t: Each audit entry links to its predecessor through hash chaining, forming a tamper-evident log (similar to Certificate Transparency ) of all agent actions.
Fields:

dl.dd: Unique event identifier. Format: evt:<uuid>.
Type of event (e.g., "intent_declared", "action_executed", "a2a_message_sent").
The agent that generated this entry.
ISO 8601 timestamp.
SHA-256 of the previous audit entry's canonical JSON. The first entry uses the sentinel value "GENESIS".
SHA3-256 of the previous entry (dual-hash chain).
SHA-256 hash of the action payload.
Protocol version string.
Session binding nonce.

section.name: Dual-Hash Chains (v2.0)
section.t: DCP v2.0 maintains parallel hash chains using SHA-256 and SHA3-256. If one hash family is cryptanalytically compromised, the other chain provides continuity and tamper evidence.

section.name: Per-Event Signing
section.t: Each audit event is signed with Ed25519 for microsecond-latency signing. This provides non-repudiation at the individual event level.

section.name: PQ Checkpoints
section.t: Every N events (configurable, default 10), a PQ checkpoint is produced: a composite signature over the Merkle root of the last N events. This amortizes the cost of post-quantum signatures across multiple events while ensuring PQ protection.

section.name: Merkle Root
section.t: The audit trail's integrity is summarized in a Merkle root computed over all audit entries. The Merkle root is included in the bundle manifest and covered by the bundle-level composite signature.

section.name: Audit Compaction
section.t: After N events (default 1000), a compaction checkpoint is produced. Compacted events MAY be archived while the checkpoint remains in the active chain, reducing storage requirements for long-running agents.

section.name: Discovery
section.t: Agents publish directories at .well-known/dcp-agent-directory.json containing agent metadata, capabilities, endpoints, and supported algorithms. Directory entries are signed by the publishing organization.

section.name: Handshake
section.t: A2A sessions are established via a four-message handshake:
section.artwork: Agent A (Initiator) Agent B (Responder) | | |-- A2A_HELLO ------------------------->| | { signed_bundle_A, kem_pk_A, | | nonce_A, supported_algs } | | | |<----------------------- A2A_WELCOME --| | { signed_bundle_B, kem_pk_B, | | nonce_B, kem_ciphertext_BA } | | | |-- A2A_CONFIRM ----------------------->| | { kem_ciphertext_AB, | | encrypted_confirm(nonce_A+B) } | | | |<------------------- A2A_ESTABLISHED --| | { encrypted_ack, session_id } | | | |== Encrypted A2A Messages ============>| |<======================================|
section.t: The handshake uses hybrid KEM (X25519 + ML-KEM-768) for post-quantum key establishment, building on the principles of . Both parties present and mutually verify their Signed Bundles before any data is exchanged.

section.name: Key Derivation
section.t: Session keys are derived using HKDF-SHA256 :
section.artwork:
shared_secret_BA = decapsulate(kem_ciphertext_BA, sk_A) shared_secret_AB = decapsulate(kem_ciphertext_AB, sk_B)
session_key = HKDF-SHA256( salt = nonce_A || nonce_B, ikm = shared_secret_BA || shared_secret_AB, info = "DCP-AI.v2.A2A.SessionKey", len = 32 )

section.name: Session Management
section.t: Established sessions use AES-256-GCM with derived session keys. Sessions maintain monotonic sequence counters, support periodic rekeying (default every 1000 messages), and generate audit entries for all A2A events.
Session termination is signaled via A2A_CLOSE with a reason code and an audit summary hash covering all session events.

section.name: Trust Model
section.t: Trust between agents is established per-session through mutual bundle verification. Trust is non-transitive by default. Transitive trust MAY be enabled with configurable depth limits and organization-scoped trust policies.

tr.td: Default for Level 3 deployments, primary hash chain

section.name: Composite Signature Binding
section.t: The pq_over_classical binding protocol ensures that neither the classical nor the post-quantum signature component can be stripped without detection:
section.artwork: Step 1: classical_sig = Classical.sign(context || 0x00 || payload) Step 2: pq_sig = PQ.sign(context || 0x00 || payload || classical_sig)
section.t: The PQ signature covers the classical signature. During verification, the verifier MUST:

ol.li: Extract classical_sig and pq_sig from the composite signature.
Verify classical_sig over context || 0x00 || payload.
Verify pq_sig over context || 0x00 || payload || classical_sig.
Reject if either verification fails.

section.t: Wire format:

section.name: Key Identifier Derivation
section.t: Key identifiers are computed deterministically from the algorithm and raw public key bytes:

section.name: Domain Separation
section.t: All signatures include a context tag to prevent cross-artifact replay attacks:
Context tags:

section.name: JSON (Default)
section.t: Content-Type: application/json. All JSON payloads MUST be canonicalized per (JCS) before signing. Canonicalization rules:

ol.li: Keys sorted lexicographically by Unicode code point.
Compact form (no whitespace).
Integers only -- floating-point values are prohibited.
No field exclusion -- the entire payload object is canonicalized.

section.name: CBOR (Optional)
section.t: Content-Type: application/cbor. Per deterministic encoding. Provides 30-40% size reduction compared to JSON. RECOMMENDED for high-throughput deployments and A2A session messages.

section.name: Bundle Manifest
section.t: The bundle manifest cryptographically binds all artifact hashes:
The bundle-level composite signature signs canonical(manifest) under the DCP-AI.v2.Bundle context tag.

section.name: Quantum Threat Mitigation
section.t: DCP uses hybrid composite signatures from day one. This defends against harvest-now-decrypt-later attacks where an adversary records signed artifacts today for cryptanalysis after a cryptographically relevant quantum computer becomes available.
The composite binding ensures that the bundle remains verifiable if either the classical or post-quantum algorithm is broken -- verification succeeds only if both components are valid.

section.name: Stripping Attack Prevention
section.t: The pq_over_classical binding prevents removal of either signature component. The post-quantum signature covers the classical signature bytes, so stripping the classical signature invalidates the PQ signature. Stripping the PQ signature is detectable because verifiers require both components in hybrid_required mode.

section.name: Cross-Protocol Replay Prevention
section.t: Domain separation tags () prevent an attacker from taking a signature produced for one artifact type (e.g., an Intent) and presenting it as valid for another (e.g., a Policy Decision). Each context tag creates a distinct signing domain.

section.name: Session Binding
section.t: The session_nonce field in v2.0 ties all artifacts in a bundle to a single session. Verifiers MUST check that all artifacts share the same nonce. This prevents session splicing attacks where artifacts from different sessions are mixed into a single bundle.

section.name: Security Tiers
section.t: Adaptive security tiers () balance performance and security based on risk context. The never-downgrade rule ensures that high-risk intents always receive maximum protection regardless of agent preferences.

section.name: Key Compromise Recovery
section.t: DCP provides multiple mechanisms for key compromise recovery:

dl.dd: Pre-registered revocation token (SHA-256 commitment). Revealing the pre-image immediately revokes all agent keys without requiring a cryptographic signature.
New keys are certified by the old key with proof-of-possession, with a configurable grace window.
Master secret split via Shamir's Secret Sharing, with shares encrypted using hybrid KEM.

section.name: Algorithm Agility
section.t: The algorithm advisory system allows governance authorities to deprecate or revoke algorithms via signed advisories. Verifiers automatically respond by removing affected algorithms, adding replacements, and transitioning to PQ-only mode when all classical algorithms are deprecated.

section.name: Canonicalization Safety
section.t: Integer-only numeric fields and the prohibition on floating-point values eliminate canonicalization ambiguity that could lead to signature validation inconsistencies across implementations.

section.name: Verification Mode Policy
section.t: Signature verification policy is set by the verifier, not the agent. This prevents an attacker from downgrading security by presenting bundles with weaker signature modes. The verifier loads its policy from its own configuration:

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document requests registration of:

tr.td: DCP Citizenship Bundle in JSON format
DCP Citizenship Bundle in CBOR format
DCP endpoint capabilities and version support
Signed revocation list for a jurisdiction
Governance key set for algorithm advisories

author.organization: National Institute of Standards and Technology
National Institute of Standards and Technology
National Institute of Standards and Technology

ul.li: Composite-bound hybrid signatures on all v2.0 artifacts
Session nonce on every artifact within a v2.0 bundle
NIST KAT validation for all PQ providers
HSM/TPM for private key storage in production
CBOR wire format for high-throughput deployments
Short-lived certificates (24h TTL) instead of revocation lists
Adaptive security tier computation for all intents
Verification result caching with tier-based TTL

section.name: Verification Pipeline
section.t: A conformant verifier MUST execute the following pipeline:

ol.li: Wire format decode (JSON or CBOR)
Schema validation (route v1/v2 by dcp_version)
Session nonce consistency check
Manifest integrity verification (recompute all artifact hashes)
Security tier resolution
Advisory-driven algorithm rejection
Composite signature verification (parallel-capable)
Tier-aware verifier policy enforcement
Audit hash chain validation (primary and secondary)
PQ checkpoint chain validation
Key validity checks (expiry, revocation, kid derivation)
Verification cache lookup/store (tier-based TTL)

section.name: Backward Compatibility Matrix
section.artwork: V1 Verifier + V1 Bundle -> PASS (unchanged) V1 Verifier + V2 Bundle -> REJECT "unsupported dcp_version 2.0" V2 Verifier + V1 Bundle -> PASS (when allow_v1_bundles=true) V2 Verifier + V2 Bundle -> PASS (full V2 verification)
section.t: V2 verifiers MUST support V1 bundles indefinitely. Migration from V1 to V2 is gradual and driven by verifier policy.
